quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 11, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Immunology: Adaptive Immunity, Lymphoid Organs, and Immune Disorders
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Humoral vs Cell-Mediated Immunity, Primary vs Secondary Lymphoid Organs, Cytokines, MHC, Interferons, Passive Immunity, Autoimmune Diseases, Allergies, HIV

questions.question: Humoral immunity primarily involves:
questions.explanation: Humoral immunity involves B cells producing antibodies that circulate in body fluids (humors). T cells are involved in cell-mediated immunity, phagocytes are innate, and skin/mucous membranes are physical barriers.

questions.question: Cell-mediated immunity involves:
questions.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity involves T cells (helper T cells, cytotoxic T cells) that directly attack infected cells. B cells and antibodies are humoral immunity, complement proteins are innate.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Vaccines work by:
questions.explanation: Vaccines stimulate adaptive immunity by presenting antigens that trigger memory cell production. They do not directly kill pathogens (that's antibiotics/antivirals), they activate adaptive (not just innate) immunity, and they do not destroy host cells.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following are examples of mechanical barriers in the immune system?
questions.explanation: Mechanical barriers physically block pathogen entry: Skin (tough, keratinized layer), mucous membranes (trap pathogens), and cilia (sweep mucus and trapped pathogens upward). Antibodies are part of adaptive immunity, and stomach acid is a chemical barrier (not mechanical).

questions.question: Which of the following immunity is present from our birth?
questions.explanation: Innate immunity is present from birth and provides non-specific, immediate defense against pathogens. Active immunity develops after exposure to pathogens or vaccination, passive immunity is temporarily acquired from antibodies (e.g., through breast milk), and acquired immunity refers to adaptive immunity that develops during life.

questions.question: B-cells and T-cells are two types of cells involved in ______.
questions.explanation: B-cells and T-cells are lymphocytes involved in acquired (adaptive) immunity. They provide specific, targeted responses with memory. Innate immunity involves phagocytes, natural killer cells, and physical barriers, not B and T cells. Active and passive immunity are types of acquired immunity, but B and T cells are specifically part of acquired immunity.

questions.question: Primary lymphoid organs include:
questions.explanation: Primary lymphoid organs are where lymphocytes mature: thymus (T cells) and bone marrow (B cells). Spleen and lymph nodes are secondary lymphoid organs where immune responses occur.

questions.question: Secondary lymphoid organs include:
questions.explanation: Secondary lymphoid organs include lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils, and Peyer's patches. They are sites where mature lymphocytes encounter antigens and initiate immune responses.

questions.question: Cytokines function to:
questions.explanation: Cytokines are signaling molecules that regulate immune responses by communicating between cells. Antibodies are produced by B cells, lysozyme digests bacterial walls, and platelets form clots.

questions.question: MHC molecules are important for:
questions.explanation: MHC (Major Histocompatibility Complex) molecules present antigen fragments to T cells. MHC I presents to cytotoxic T cells, MHC II to helper T cells. This is crucial for adaptive immunity.

questions.question: Interferons are released primarily in response to:
questions.explanation: Interferons are antiviral proteins released by virus-infected cells to warn neighboring cells and inhibit viral replication. They are part of innate immunity against viruses.

questions.question: Passive immunity can be acquired through:
questions.explanation: Passive immunity involves receiving pre-formed antibodies, e.g., through breast milk (maternal antibodies) or antibody injections. Vaccines and infection provide active immunity (body produces its own antibodies).

questions.question: Autoimmune diseases occur when:
questions.explanation: Autoimmune diseases result from the immune system mistakenly attacking self-tissues (e.g., type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus). Immune deficiency is failure to respond, complement absence causes specific deficiencies.

questions.question: Allergies are caused by:
questions.explanation: Allergies are hypersensitivity reactions where the immune system overreacts to harmless antigens (allergens), often involving IgE antibodies and mast cell degranulation.

questions.question: HIV primarily targets:
questions.explanation: HIV targets CD4+ helper T cells, leading to their depletion and immunodeficiency. It can also infect macrophages but helper T cells are the primary target. This cripples adaptive immunity.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered adaptive immunity and immune disorders! 🛡️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between primary and secondary lymphoid organs.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on humoral vs cell-mediated immunity and HIV targets.
scoreMessages.0: Please review B cell/T cell functions, MHC presentation, and autoimmune diseases.
